Jul 19 2019

<div class="blog-header">

# Compounding Relationships Make Life Easier

<div class="blog-subtitle">

Life gets a lot easier when you know someone’s got your back

</div>

<div class="podcast-container style-1">

<div class="audio-player-container">

<div class="controls">

<a href="#" class="not-smooth play-pause-btn"></a>

<div class="dropdown">

<div class="date">

Jul 19 2019

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="content">

**Nivi:** Earlier, we talked about [compound
interest](https://nav.al/long-term), but we didn’t dig into it much.

**Naval:** Relationships offer a good example of compound interest. Once
you’ve been in a good relationship with somebody for a while—whether
it’s business or romantic—life gets a lot easier because you know that
person’s got your back. You don’t have to keep questioning.

If I’m doing a deal with someone I’ve worked with for 20 years and there
is mutual trust, we don’t have to read the legal contracts. Maybe we
don’t even need to *create* legal contracts; maybe we can do it with a
handshake. That kind of trust makes it very easy to do business.

<span id="more-28493317"></span>

**Mutual trust makes it easy to do business**

<span style="">If Nivi and I start another company and things aren’t
working out, I know we’re both going to be extremely reasonable about
deciding what to do—how to exit or shut it down. Or if we’re scaling it,
how to bring in new people. We have mutual trust, and that allows us to
start businesses more easily and compounds the effect. </span>

<span style="">The most under-recognized reason startups fail is because
the founders fall apart. </span>

<span style="">A startup is so difficult to pull off, so removing
potential friction points between founders can be the difference between
success and failure. </span>

**It’s better to have a few compounding relationships than many shallow
ones **

**Nivi:**<span style=""> There are a couple of non-intuitive things
about compounding. The first is that most of the benefits come at the
end, so you may not see huge benefits up front. </span>

<span style="">Sam Altman wrote, “</span>[<span style="">I always want
it to be a project that, if successful, will make the rest of my career
look like a
footnote</span>](http://blog.samaltman.com/how-to-be-successful)<span style="">.”
Again, most of the benefits of compounding come at the end.</span>

<span style="">Another thing that’s non-intuitive about compounding:
It’s better to have a few deep compounding relationships than many
shallow, non-compounding relationships. </span>

**It takes just as much effort to create a small business as a large
one**

**Naval:**<span style=""> One thing about business that people don’t
realize: it takes just as much effort to create a small business as it
does to create a large one. </span>

<span style="">Whether you’re Elon Musk or the guy running three Italian
restaurants in town, you’re working 80 hours a week; you’re sweating
bullets; you’re hiring and firing people; you’re trying to balance the
books; it’s highly stressful; and it takes years and years of your
life. </span>

<span style="">In one case, you get companies worth \$50-\$100 billion
and everyone’s adulation. In the other, you might make a little bit of
money and you’ve got some nice restaurants. So think big.</span>
